dialogues: You've been using the commutative property to solve problems, with arrays, without arrays, in all kinds of situations. Let's step back and connect what you discovered.
Two ways to prove the same thing. On the left, highlighting. On the right, rotation. What's the SAME about both?
Right. Same dots. That's the anchor. Whether you read it a different way or turn it, no dots change. Same dots means same total. Always.
Here's one you haven't seen as an array today. 9 times 7 is 63. What's 7 times 9?
Right, 63. You didn't need an array. You used the turn-around facts.
Now here's the big question. Will this work for ANY two numbers you multiply, not just the ones we tried?

Exactly. Any two numbers. You could build an array for 9 × 7 and rotate it. Same dots, same product. You can imagine an array even when you don't see one. That's why mathematicians call it a property. It's always true.

And here's something interesting. This isn't true for every operation. 8 minus 3 is NOT the same as 3 minus 8. But with multiplication, you can always switch the factors and get the same product. You already knew addition worked this way: 3 plus 5 equals 5 plus 3. Now you've proven multiplication works the same way.
Look at this. You learned 3 facts. But because you can multiply the factors in any order, you actually know 6 facts. Every fact gives you its partner for free.
Which of these is a good way to use this strategy?
That's it. No extra work. One fact, two answers. That's what makes this a strategy, it saves you time and effort.
If you had to remember ONE thing from today, what would it be?
Right. That's the big idea. The order doesn't change the product and you proved it.
That's where it all started. Two descriptions, same array and same product.
Exactly. Rotation made it impossible to deny. Same dots, just turned.
That's the strategy. One fact, two answers. That saves you real time.

Think about where you started. You found equal groups in bags and circles. You learned that multiplication means groups of the same size. You built expressions, then equations. You discovered arrays: organized groups with rows and columns. And today, you proved something powerful: you can multiply in any order and always get the same answer.
You didn't just memorize that. You proved it with highlighting and rotation. You used it as a strategy. That's real mathematical thinking.
